twitter_thread: 85% of companies waste $1.2m/year on duplicate AI work due to poor skill sharing.
Sx 2.0 uses a Dropbox folder to share AI skills, but 40% of teams use GitHub instead.
Named entity recognition and sentiment analysis are 2x harder to share than computer vision skills.
Storing AI models on a shared drive can lead to 500+ version control issues/month, avoid this.
Sharing AI skills via code snippets and Jupyter notebooks can reduce onboarding time by 30%.
Combining TensorFlow, PyTorch, and scikit-learn can improve AI model accuracy by 25%.
What's the most effective way you've found to share AI skills with your team? #shareai #aiteam
pack.linkedin: Companies waste $1.2m/year on duplicate AI work due to poor skill sharing. Approaches like Sx 2.0, which uses a Dropbox folder, and GitHub, used by 40% of teams, are emerging. Sharing named entity recognition and sentiment analysis skills is particularly challenging, with 2x the difficulty of computer vision skills. Using a shared drive can lead to 500+ version control issues/month, while code snippets and Jupyter notebooks can reduce onboarding time by 30%. What methods have you found to be most effective in sharing AI skills with your team?
tiktok_hooks: AI teams wasting $1.2m/year on duplicate work, fix this now
2x harder to share some AI skills, what's your solution
25% boost in AI model accuracy, how are you achieving this
pack.reddit_headline: Is Share AI a $1.2m/year problem or a minor inconvenience?
